The good examples of other Christians and churches should encourage us. It is good to save resources for helpful purposes. Those who are wealthy in this world should be rich in good works. Diligence will not make someone rich without God’s blessing. And what is more likely to inspire us to charity toward the people and children of God, than to view all we have as his gift? Acts of mercy are real results of true love for God, and therefore appropriate actions on his day. Ministers do their proper work when they promote or help works of charity. A Christian minister’s heart must be with the people among whom he has worked long and successfully. All our plans should be made with submission to God's care. Opposition does not break the spirits of faithful and successful ministers, but strengthens their zeal and inspires them with fresh courage. A faithful minister is more discouraged by the hardness of his listeners’ hearts and the backsliding of those who claim to be Christians than by enemies’ attempts.

Timothy came to do the work of the Lord. Therefore, upsetting his spirit would grieve the Holy Spirit; rejecting him would reject Him that sent him. Those who do the work of the Lord should be treated with kindness and respect. Faithful ministers will not be jealous of each other. It is fitting for gospel ministers to show concern for each other’s reputation and usefulness.

A Christian is always in danger, therefore should always be watchful. He should remain steadfast in the faith of the gospel and never abandon or give it up. By this faith alone he will be able to stand firm during times of temptation. Christians should ensure that charity not only exists in their hearts but shines in their lives. There is a great difference between Christian strength and feverish excitement. The apostle gave specific instructions regarding some who served the cause of Christ among them. Those who serve the saints, those who desire the honor of the churches, and to remove reproach from them, are to be highly regarded and loved. They should willingly acknowledge the worth of such people and all who worked with or helped the apostle.

Christianity does not destroy politeness. Religion should promote a courteous and helpful attitude toward everyone. Those who use religion as an excuse to be sour and gloomy give a false impression of it and reproach it. And Christian greetings are not mere empty compliments, but real expressions of goodwill to others, commending them to God’s grace and blessing. Every Christian family should be like a Christian church. Wherever two or three are gathered together in the name of Christ, and he is among them, there is a church. Here is a serious warning. Many who speak much about Christ’s name have no true love for him in their hearts. No one loves him truly if they do not love his laws and keep his commandments. Many claim to be Christians but do not sincerely love the Lord Jesus Christ. Such people are separated from God's people and his favor. Those who do not love the Lord Jesus Christ will perish without hope. Let us not settle for any religious profession where there is no love of Christ, earnest desires for his salvation, gratitude for his mercies, and obedience to his commandments. The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ contains all that is good, for time and eternity. To wish that our friends may have this grace with them is wishing them the greatest possible blessing. And this we should wish all our friends and brothers in Christ. We can wish them nothing greater, nor anything less. True Christianity makes us want those whom we love to have the blessings of both worlds; this is what it means to wish for the grace of Christ to be with them. The apostle spoke plainly to the Corinthians, telling them of their faults with just severity, but he departs in love and with a solemn declaration of his love for them for Christ’s sake. May our love be with all who are in Christ Jesus. Let us examine whether everything seems worthless to us when compared with Christ and his righteousness. Do we allow ourselves in any known sin or neglect any known duty? By such inquiries, faithfully made, we may judge the state of our souls.
